metadata.subject: Science
metadata.description: Anatomy and Physiology 12 is an advanced senior biology course examining how the human body is organized and maintained at molecular, cellular, and systemic levels. Students explore homeostasis, gene expression and protein synthesis, and the complex interrelationships between organ systems. The course integrates holistic health perspectives — including First Peoples approaches to well-being — alongside Western anatomical and physiological frameworks. It is foundational for health science pathways.
metadata.general_description: An advanced senior course examining human anatomy and physiology through the lenses of homeostasis, gene expression, and organ system interrelationships — integrating Western biomedical science with holistic and First Peoples health perspectives for health science pathways.

big_ideas.statement: Homeostasis is maintained through physiological processes.
big_ideas.elaboration: The human body maintains stable internal conditions — temperature, pH, blood glucose, fluid balance — through continuous feedback mechanisms. Disruption of these homeostatic processes is the basis of disease.

big_ideas.statement: Gene expression, through protein synthesis, is an interaction between genes and the environment.
big_ideas.elaboration: DNA is not destiny — gene expression is regulated by internal and external environmental signals. Epigenetics, post-translational modification, and genomic variation all influence how genes are expressed in different tissues and conditions.

big_ideas.statement: Organ systems have complex interrelationships to maintain homeostasis.
big_ideas.elaboration: No organ system works in isolation. The nervous, endocrine, cardiovascular, immune, and other systems continuously interact and regulate one another — a level of integration that is essential to understanding how the body functions in health and disease.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate sustained intellectual curiosity; formulate multiple hypotheses about scientific topics.
i_can_statements: I can formulate complex questions about human anatomy, physiology, and health.
I can generate multiple hypotheses about homeostatic mechanisms and organ system interactions.
I can predict the physiological consequences of disruptions to homeostasis.

competencies.raw: Collaboratively design investigations using appropriate methods and SI units while assessing risks.
i_can_statements: I can design investigations about physiological processes using appropriate ethical standards and methods.
I can conduct physiological measurements (blood pressure, heart rate, lung capacity) safely and accurately.
I can analyze anatomical specimens and models with appropriate methods and respect.

categories.name: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information
categories.raw: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information

competencies.raw: Seek patterns in data; construct graphs; draw evidence-based conclusions.
i_can_statements: I can identify patterns in physiological data and connect them to underlying biological mechanisms.
I can construct and interpret diagrams of feedback loops, metabolic pathways, and organ system interactions.
I can draw evidence-based conclusions about health, disease, and physiological function from data.

competencies.raw: Assess methods for errors; evaluate model validity; critically analyze information sources.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate the validity of anatomical and physiological models and their limitations.
I can critically analyze health information sources including medical research and media claims.
I can consider the ethical implications of biotechnology, genomics, and medical research.

competencies.raw: Implement strategies to solve problems with local or global applications.
i_can_statements: I can apply anatomical and physiological knowledge to understand health conditions and treatments.
I can connect my learning to health science careers and real-world medical and public health challenges.
I can consider Indigenous healing practices alongside biomedical approaches to health and well-being.

competencies.raw: Present scientific ideas using evidence-based arguments and appropriate scientific language.
i_can_statements: I can communicate anatomical and physiological concepts using correct scientific terminology.
I can present evidence-based arguments about health, disease, and physiological mechanisms.
I can create diagrams, models, and reports that represent anatomical and physiological systems accurately.

areas.name: Biochemistry and Cell Physiology
areas.raw: Biological molecules, metabolism and enzymes, transport across cell membranes.
topics: Biological macromolecules: carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, nucleic acids — structure and function
Enzymes: structure, active site, denaturation, cofactors, and inhibition
Metabolic pathways: anabolism, catabolism, and ATP production overview
Transport across cell membranes: passive (diffusion, osmosis) and active transport
Cell signalling: receptors, signal transduction pathways, and cellular responses
Feedback loops: negative and positive feedback in homeostasis

areas.name: Gene Expression and Genomics
areas.raw: Gene expression through protein synthesis as interaction between genes and environment.
topics: DNA structure, replication, and repair mechanisms
Transcription and translation: detailed mechanisms and regulation
Gene regulation: promoters, transcription factors, operons, and epigenetics
Epigenetic modifications: methylation, histone modification, and environmental influence on gene expression
Genomics: genome sequencing, comparative genomics, and the human genome project
Biotechnology: CRISPR-Cas9, gene therapy, recombinant DNA, and personalized medicine
Ethical considerations in genomics and genetic technology

areas.name: Organ Systems and Homeostasis
areas.raw: Organ systems with complex interrelationships maintaining homeostasis.
topics: Organization: cells → tissues (epithelial, connective, muscle, nervous) → organs → systems
Integumentary system: skin layers, thermoregulation, and protection
Nervous system: central and peripheral divisions, neurons, synaptic transmission, reflexes
Endocrine system: glands, hormones, feedback loops, and coordination with nervous system
Cardiovascular system: heart anatomy, cardiac cycle, blood vessels, blood pressure regulation
Respiratory system: anatomy, gas exchange, ventilation mechanics, pH regulation
Immune system: innate and adaptive immunity, antibodies, vaccines, and autoimmunity
Digestive system: organs, enzymes, nutrient absorption, and gut microbiome
Excretory system: kidney structure, nephron function, filtration, reabsorption, and osmoregulation
Musculoskeletal system: bone, muscle contraction, sliding filament theory
Reproductive system: anatomy, hormonal cycles, fertilization, embryonic development overview

areas.name: Health, Disease, and Holistic Perspectives
areas.raw: Lifestyle factors, holistic health approaches, and disease as homeostatic imbalance.
topics: Disease as disruption of homeostasis: examples from each organ system
Lifestyle factors: exercise, nutrition, sleep, stress, and their physiological effects
Chronic diseases: diabetes, cardiovascular disease, cancer — mechanisms and prevention
Mental health: neurological basis, stress response, and the brain-body connection
First Peoples holistic health models: physical, emotional, mental, and spiritual well-being
Traditional plant medicine and ethnopharmacology in BC First Peoples health traditions
Health equity: determinants of health, systemic barriers, and Indigenous health outcomes
